text: In this video, we present you a short walkthrough of a laparoscopic anterior rectal resection with special emphasis on the assessment of the perfusion of the anastomosis using fluorescence endoscopic imaging. Patient is a forty-five year old Caucasian female with an adenocarcinoma of the rectum eight centimeters from the dentate line. MRI was performed for local staging. There is a single enlarged lymph node. The tumor is restricted to the submucosa, resulting in a T-one N-plus classification, which was also confirmed by an endorectal ultrasound.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the left colon using a medial to lateral approach, exposing the feeding artery and draining vein. The left ureter and gonadal vein are preserved, while the feeding vessels are clipped and severed. The lateral mobilization extends to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon enters the mesorectal fascia dorsally, sparing the pelvic nerves, and performs a total mesorectal excision in a circular fashion. A small incision is made in the lower left quadrant to assess perfusion traditionally by observing tissue color and palpating the pulse in the marginal artery. The lighting is switched off, and indocyanine green is administered intravenously, revealing sufficient perfusion through the green fluorescence visible in the colon and mesenteric vessels.

Following extracorporeal bowel division, the surgeon closes the small incision and re-establishes pneumoperitoneum. A standard stapler anastomosis is performed, followed by a leakage test, which shows no leakage. Perfusion is reassessed using an endoscopic view, highlighting the vascular supply to the anastomosis.
Post-operative course was uneventful. The patient was discharged on day five. No adjuvant therapy was necessary.
